title: On the Resilience of LLM-Based Multi-Agent Collaboration with Faulty Agents
abstract: Large language model-based multi-agent systems have shown great abilities across various tasks due to the collaboration of expert agents, each focusing on a specific domain. However, the impact of clumsy or even malicious agents-those who frequently make errors in their tasks-on the overall performance of the system remains underexplored. This paper investigates: (1) What is the resilience of various system structures (e.g., A→B→C, A↔B↔C) under faulty agents, on different downstream tasks? (2) How can we increase system resilience to defend against these agents? To simulate faulty agents, we propose two approaches-AUTOTRANSFORM and AU- TOINJECT-which introduce mistakes into the agents’ responses. Experiments on four downstream tasks using six systems show that the “hierarchical” structure, i.e., A→(B↔C), exhibits superior resilience with the lowest performance drop of 5.5%, compared to 10.5% and 23.7% of other two structures. To further improve resilience, we introduce (1) Challenger, that introduces a mechanism for each agent to challenge others’ outputs, and (2) Inspector, an additional agent to review and correct messages, recovering up to 96.4% errors made by faulty agents. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/ CUHK-ARISE/MAS-Resilience.
impact: The two error-introducing methods developed in this study, AUTOTRANSFORM and AUTOINJECT, could potentially pollute benign agents and result in negative social impacts. To mitigate this risk, we have proposed effective defense mechanisms, the Challenger and the Inspector, against them. We would like to emphasize that the goal of proposing these methodologies is to study and improve the behavior of LLMbased multi-agent collaboration. We strongly oppose any malicious use of these methods to achieve negative ends.

query_input.scenario: Multi-agent LLM frameworks with fine-tuned component agents
query_input.issue: Backdoored agent triggers malicious actions, bypassing collaboration safeguards
hazard: Multi-agent LLM systems with hierarchical error correction still risk propagating undetected faulty or malicious agent behaviors.
exposure: End-users of multi-agent personal assistant applications

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Users lack independent verification tools and blindly trust collaboration safeguards that backdoored agents bypass.
hipporag_result.impact: Users experience unauthorized actions, data breaches, or harmful decisions from compromised personal assistants.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Through end-users of personal assistant applications who lack independent verification and blindly trust collaboration safeguards, multi-agent LLM systems with hierarchical error correction propagate undetected malicious agent behaviors, leading to unauthorized actions, data breaches, or harmful decisions.

dose_response: Multi-agent LLM systems with undetected backdoored fine-tuned agents trigger malicious actions in personal assistant applications, causing user data theft and privacy violations through lack of agent integrity checks.
vulnerability: Backdoor triggers in fine-tuned agents activate malicious behavior.
Absence of agent integrity checks allows compromised agents to join.
Collaboration protocols trust all agents, enabling lateral compromise.
impact: User data theft and privacy violations via malicious agent actions.
Widespread manipulation of personal assistant outputs harming users.
Reputational damage to AI assistant providers and loss of user trust.
